back_translation: After these events, Paul left the city of Athens and went to the city of Corinth.

There he met a certain Jew named Aquila, who was a native of the city of Pontus by birth, who had recently come from Italy with Priscilla his wife, because Claudius had issued an imperial decree for all the Jews to leave the city of Rome. So Paul went to visit them,
and because Paul had the same trade as they did, he stayed and worked with them, for they were tentmakers by trade.
Every Sabbath day he reasoned and disputed in the synagogue, and tried to persuade both Jews and Greeks.
When Silas and Timothy came down from the province of Macedonia, Paul was constrained by the word, testifying to the Jews that Jesus is the Christ.
But when they opposed and uttered blasphemies, Paul shook out his garments and said to them, 'Let your blood fall upon your own heads! I am innocent. From now on I will go to the Gentiles.'
Then Paul left there and entered the house of a man named Titius Justus, who was a God-fearer. His house was next door to the synagogue.
Crispus the synagogue leader believed in the Lord together with his whole household. And many of the Corinthians who heard believed and were baptized.
One night the Lord spoke to Paul through a vision, saying, 'Do not be afraid, but continue to speak and do not be silent,
for I am with you, and no one will harm you, because in this city I have many people.'
So Paul stayed there one year and six months, teaching the word of God among them.
When Gallio was the proconsul of the province of Achaia, the Jewish leaders united in rising up against Paul and brought him to the judgment-throne,
saying, 'This man persuades people to worship God contrary to the law.'
But when Paul was about to open his mouth, Gallio said to the Jews, 'O Jews, if this matter were some unjust act or a serious crime, I would reasonably listen to you patiently.
But since it is a dispute about words, names, and your own law, you go and handle it yourselves. I do not want to be a judge of these matters.'
Then he drove them away from the judgment-throne.
Then all of them seized Sosthenes the synagogue leader and beat him in front of the judgment-throne, but Gallio was not at all concerned about these things.

Paul stayed with the brothers for many more days, then he said farewell to them and sailed for the province of Syria, together with Priscilla and Aquila. At the city of Cenchrea he had his head shaved, because he had made a vow.
When they reached the city of Ephesus, Paul left Priscilla and Aquila there. He himself entered the synagogue and reasoned with the Jews.
When they asked him to stay longer, he did not consent.
But he said farewell and declared, 'I will return to you again, if God is pleased to will it.' Then he set sail from Ephesus.
When he landed at the city of Caesarea, he went up [to Jerusalem] and greeted the church, then went down to the city of Antioch.
After he had stayed there for some time, he set out traveling in succession through the province of Galatia and the province of Phrygia, strengthening all the disciples.
There was a certain Jew named Apollos, a native of Alexandria by birth, a man skilled in speech, who came to the city of Ephesus. He was one who had expertise in the Scriptures.
He had been instructed in the way of the Lord, and with fervent spirit he spoke and taught accurately about Jesus, although he knew only the baptism of John.
He began to proclaim boldly in the synagogue, and when Priscilla and Aquila heard him, they took him in and explained the way of God to him more accurately.

When Apollos resolved to cross over to the province of Achaia, the brothers encouraged him and wrote to the disciples there to welcome him. When he arrived he greatly helped those who had believed by grace,
for he vigorously refuted the Jews publicly, proving from the Scriptures that Jesus is the Christ.
